claims.claim: Frontmatter code_ref deepgram.go:92 shows keyword/keyterm STT param handling
claims.found: line 92: `if keywordsRaw, exists := dgOpt.mdlOpts["listen.keyword"]; exists {` — start of the keyword/keyterm block (92-117)

claims.claim: deepgram.go:92-117 branches on whether config specifies `keyword` vs `keyterm` model params
claims.found: Block reads a SINGLE config key `listen.keyword`; then branches on opts.Model: line 109 `if opts.Model == "nova-2" { opts.Keywords = keywords }`, line 112 `if opts.Model == "nova-3" { opts.Keyterm = keywords }`. The branch is on model name, not a 'keyword vs keyterm' config param. Cited line range is correct; mechanism phrasing is imprecise.
claims.correction: deepgram.go:109-114 — branches on opts.Model (nova-2 -> opts.Keywords, nova-3 -> opts.Keyterm) from the single listen.keyword input

claims.claim: stt_callback.go:52-143 handles is_final emits independent of language (stream is language-agnostic)
claims.found: Message() 52-143: control flow branches on confidence threshold (60-79) and `if mr.IsFinal {` (81), never on language. Language extracted via GetMostUsedLanguage (57) and attached as packet metadata only; no language-conditional routing.

claims.claim: Deepgram defines two multilingual strategies: unified multilingual conversational streams and language-specialized streams (~808-818)
claims.found: 'Unified multilingual conversational streams, where a single real-time stream supports multiple languages dynamically' and 'Language-specialized conversational streams, where the system converges on a dominant language and optimizes for it over time'

claims.claim: Unified streams 'prioritize continuity. They avoid disruptive mid-conversation transitions, simplify orchestration, and handle moderate code-switching naturally' (~822-825)
claims.found: 'Unified multilingual streams prioritize continuity. They avoid disruptive mid-conversation transitions, simplify orchestration, and handle moderate code-switching naturally.' — verbatim

claims.claim: Example: customer support bot 'responds in Spanish, then seamlessly switches to French if the user changes languages mid-conversation' (~816)
claims.found: '...a customer support bot' / 'that responds in Spanish, then seamlessly switches to French if the user' / 'changes languages mid-conversation' — verbatim (two-column wrap)

claims.claim: Deepgram advises continuity usually matters more than marginal accuracy gains unless regulatory/domain/cost considerations dictate otherwise (~824-827)
claims.found: 'In practice, real-time voice agents benefit most from minimizing disruption. Continuity usually matters more than marginal accuracy gains unless regulatory, domain-specific, or cost considerations dictate otherwise.' — verbatim

claims.claim: Language Coach pattern: Nova-3 Multilingual real-time transcription in one continuous session, code-switching without reinitialization (~1429-1455)
claims.found: Header 1429 'Language Coach (Multilingual and Localization Pattern)'; body 'Nova-3 Multilingual handles real-time transcription across languages within one continuous session, enabling natural code-switching without reinitialization.'

claims.claim: Cross-note links (LANG-02/03/04, STT-01) listed as related
claims.found: Links line lists neighbors; note does not assert any neighbor proves a specific claim — nothing to refute.

summary: All deepgram-ebook quotes verified verbatim against the ground-truth parsed PDF (two-column layout; cited ~line numbers approximate but text exact). Both cited code files exist at the stated lines. One inline mechanism claim is MISCITED/imprecise: deepgram.go:92-117 reads a single `listen.keyword` config key and branches on opts.Model (nova-2 -> Keywords vs nova-3 -> Keyterm), not on a 'keyword vs keyterm config param' as worded. The 'language-agnostic emit' claim holds (control flow gates on confidence/IsFinal, never language). No WRONG claims, one MISCITED -> PARTIAL. (A prior reviewer marked PASS; I downgrade to PARTIAL on the mechanism-wording imprecision.)
